title: Arrays & Strings
description: Master 1D and 2D arrays, C-strings, and the std::string class from a competitive programmer's lens. Learn the array patterns that appear in 60% of Codeforces Div.2 A/B problems — frequency arrays, prefix sums, and the two-pointer technique — plus string manipulation tricks that save minutes in contests.

sections.content: In competitive programming, **arrays are the first tool you reach for**. They offer O(1) indexed access and predictable cache behavior, making them faster than most alternatives for dense data. The key is knowing all the declaration and initialization forms — wrong initialization is one of the most common sources of bugs in contests.

sections.title: Array Declaration, Init & Access

sections.label: Stack vs Global Arrays
sections.content: Stack memory is limited (~1–8 MB depending on OS/judge). For arrays of size > 10⁶, **always declare globally**. Global arrays are stored in the BSS/data segment and automatically zero-initialized, which eliminates a common source of bugs.
🔢 Frequency Arrays — Pattern You'll Use Daily

A frequency (count) array maps values to their occurrences. This O(n) technique replaces sorting in many problems and is the foundation of counting sort. It's the first optimization you should consider when the value range is small (≤ 10⁶).

sections.title: Frequency Array — Counting & Querying

sections.content: Two-dimensional arrays represent **grids, matrices, and adjacency matrices**. A huge class of CP problems — pathfinding, flood fill, matrix operations — live here. The critical habit: always index consistently and never go out of bounds.

sections.title: 2D Array — Declaration, Input, Traversal

sections.label: Direction Array Trick
sections.content: The `dx[]`/`dy[]` direction array is the standard CP idiom for grid traversal. For 8-directional (including diagonals), just extend both arrays to 8 entries. This pattern appears in virtually every grid-based problem.

C++ gives you two string types. **C-strings** (`char[]`) are raw character arrays terminated by `\0`. **`std::string`** is a class wrapping dynamic character storage. In competitive programming, `std::string` is almost always preferred — it's safer, more flexible, and barely slower for most problem constraints.

sections.title: std::string — Essential Operations

sections.label: strlen() is O(n) — A Hidden TLE Source
sections.content: If you use a C-string, calling `strlen(s)` inside a loop is O(n) per call, making your loop O(n²). Always store the length: `int n = strlen(s);` once before the loop. `std::string::size()` has no this problem — it returns a cached value in O(1).

sections.title: Palindrome Check & Anagram Detection
Pattern: Sliding Window Maximum
Pattern: Difference Array (Range Update in O(1))
Exercise 1: Array Initialization Trap
sections.explanation: `int a[5] = {1, 2}` sets a[0]=1, a[1]=2, and zero-fills the rest: a[2]=0, a[4]=0. `int b[5] = {}` zero-initializes all elements. After `fill(a, a+5, 9)`, every element of a becomes 9.

sections.title: Exercise 2: String Operations Chain
sections.explanation: `"hello"` has 5 chars. After `+= " world"`, it's 11 chars. `substr(6,5)` = `"world"` (starting at index 6, 5 chars). Reversed: `"dlrow olleh"`. After sort, space (' ') comes first (ASCII 32) and 'w' last (ASCII 119), so `s[0]=' '` and `s.back()='w'`.

sections.title: Exercise 3: 2D Array Traversal Output
Exercise 4: Reverse Words in a String
sections.approach: Split by spaces into a vector of strings, then print the vector in reverse. Use `stringstream` to tokenize, then iterate from `size()-1` to 0. This is O(n) time and space.
sections.explanation: `getline` reads the full line. `stringstream` tokenizes by whitespace. Storing words in a vector allows reverse traversal. We print a space between words (not after the last) to avoid trailing spaces, which can cause Wrong Answer on strict judges.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Frequency Count — Find Missing Number
sections.approach: Two clean O(n) approaches: (1) Math: expected sum = n*(n+1)/2, subtract actual sum. (2) XOR: XOR all indices and values — missing number is the result. Both avoid frequency arrays entirely.
sections.explanation: Sum of 1..n = n*(n+1)/2. The array has n-1 elements with one missing. The missing number = expected sum − actual sum. Use `long long` because n*(n+1)/2 can overflow `int` for n=10^6 (result ≈ 5×10^11).
